itineraries.title: Central Thailand: Bangkok, Ancient Kingdoms & the River Kwai
itineraries.subtitle: From Bangkok's gilded temples to Ayutthaya's haunting ruins, from the Bridge on the River Kwai to Erawan's turquoise cascades - 14 days through the heart of historic Thailand.
itineraries.notes: This itinerary passes through several UNESCO World Heritage historical parks. Amphawa Floating Market operates only Friday, Saturday, and Sunday - verify days according to your schedule. The Death Railway train (Kanchanaburi-Nam Tok) runs only twice daily from Kanchanaburi.

visits.text: Enter the Grand Palace through the Mani Noppharat Gate (new entrance since January 2024). This 54-acre complex houses Wat Phra Kaew and its Emerald Buddha - a 24-inch jade sculpture draped in gold that the king himself redresses three times yearly according to the season. Interior galleries unfold murals of the Ramakien, the Thai version of the Ramayana, across nearly a mile of walls. Allow at least 2 hours. Cover shoulders and legs.

Exit the Grand Palace and walk 10 minutes to Wat Pho. Step into the main chapel: the Reclining Buddha stretches 151 feet long and 49 feet high, entirely covered in gold leaf. His feet are inlaid with mother-of-pearl depicting the 108 auspicious characteristics of Buddha. The temple has also housed since 1962 the Thai massage school that has trained over 200,000 practitioners.

Take the ferry from Tha Tien pier (4 baht) to cross the Chao Phraya. On the opposite bank, Wat Arun reveals itself: its central prang tower rises 269 feet, encrusted with colored porcelain fragments once donated by Bangkok residents. Climb the steep steps for the river view. Late afternoon, the light sets the mosaics ablaze - it's the best time to visit.

activities.text: In the evening, take the MRT to Wat Mangkon station and dive into Chinatown. From 6 PM onward, Yaowarat Road transforms into an open-air food hall. Order grilled prawns or stir-fried noodles directly from a street stall, perched on a red plastic stool. The smell of durian will guide you better than any map.

visits.text: Push open the door to Jim Thompson's House in the Siam district. This former OSS agent reconstructed six traditional teak houses purchased from Ayutthaya, then assembled them here in 1959 along a canal. He filled them with a collection of Thai, Burmese, and Cambodian Buddhist art before mysteriously vanishing in Malaysia in 1967. The guided tour lasts about 90 minutes.

activities.text: In the afternoon, board a longtail boat tour of the Thonburi klongs. In one hour, you glide past wooden stilt houses, hidden temples, and gardens spilling into the canals. This is Bangkok before the skyscrapers. Tour operators offer these circuits from Tha Chang pier.

In the evening, ascend to a Bangkok rooftop - the Silom or Sathorn districts concentrate the best venues. From 650 to 1,000 feet high, the skyline ignites and the curve of the Chao Phraya traces itself through the darkness. Reserve ahead during high season.

visits.text: On weekends, head to Chatuchak Market - 15,000 stalls spread across 35 acres, 200,000 visitors daily. It's the world's largest weekend market. Take the BTS to Mo Chit or the MRT to Chatuchak Park. Arrive before 10 AM: after noon, heat and crowds make navigation difficult. Browse through sections for textiles, ceramics, vintage goods, and plants.

In the afternoon, descend to Lumphini Park - 143 acres of greenery in the heart of the business district. This Bangkok green lung hosts a colony of roughly 400 water monitors that bask by the lake or swim calmly. These lizards, which can exceed 6 feet in length, supremely ignore visitors. Watch them from the bridges on the park's north side.

activities.text: In the evening, pass through Khao San Road - the legendary backpacker street, 500 meters of bars, street stalls, tattoo parlors, and fried insect vendors. This isn't authentic Thailand, but it's an experience unto itself. Sample pad thai with prawns from a street stall before joining the scene.

On weekdays when not visiting Chatuchak, explore Tha Prachan Market instead, the Buddhist amulet market along the river. Thai locals come here to purchase protective statues and talismans. Atmosphere 100% local.

visits.text: Explore the creative district of Charoen Krung and its Talad Noi alleyways - former warehouses converted into art galleries, design cafés, and workshops. It's one of Bangkok's most photogenic neighborhoods, far from standard tourist circuits. Brick facades and street art neighbor 19th-century Chinese merchant houses.

Next, head to Wat Traimit in Chinatown. It houses the world's largest solid gold Buddha - a 10-foot statue weighing 5.5 tons of pure gold. It was discovered by accident in 1955 beneath a plaster coating during relocation. The 4th-floor museum traces the story of this discovery.

activities.text: Book a Thai cooking class at one of the many central workshops. In 3 to 4 hours, you prepare pad thai, tom yum soup, and green curry from scratch, after a local market visit to select ingredients. Providers offer these classes in French or English.

Treat yourself to a traditional Thai massage - 60 to 90 minutes on a floor mat, dressed in pajamas. Practitioners work energy lines with stretches and pressure. Wat Pho offers sessions performed by students from its massage school.

visits.text: Take the train from Bangkok (departure from Hua Lamphong or Krung Thep Aphiwat, approximately 90 minutes). Upon arriving in Ayutthaya, rent a bicycle - it's the best way to navigate between ruins. Begin with Wat Mahathat, founded in 1374. It's world-famous for its Buddha head - decapitated during the 1767 Burmese invasion - that became trapped in the roots of a bodhi tree. Legend says the tree caught and cradled it as it rolled there. You kneel to photograph it.

Cross the square to Wat Phra Si Sanphet. This was the kingdom's largest temple, reserved for royal use exclusively. Its three bell-shaped chedis, dating from the 15th and 16th centuries, house the ashes of three Ayutthaya sovereigns. At its peak, a 52-foot gilded Buddha statue stood here - it was melted down by the Burmese in 1767 to recover its 756 pounds of gold.

Just beside it, enter Wihan Phra Mongkhon Bophit - a still-active temple housing one of the country's largest bronze Buddha statues, standing 40.8 feet tall. Unlike neighboring ruins, this temple breathes with life: monks meditate here, incense burns, jasmine flowers are offered.

activities.text: In the afternoon, explore Ayutthaya island by bicycle. The archaeological site spans 714 acres surrounded by three rivers. Pedal between isolated prangs, decapitated Buddhas aligned along galleries, lichen-covered stupas. Wat Ratchaburana warrants a stop: climb into the prang and descend into the underground crypt where 15th-century frescoes survived.

visits.text: Rise before 6:30 AM and cycle toward Wat Chaiwatthanaram on the west bank of the Chao Phraya River. Built in 1630 by King Prasat Thong, this Khmer-style temple aligns a 115-foot central prang surrounded by eight chedis connected by a gallery of Buddhas - nearly all decapitated. At sunrise, the golden raking light on the laterite silhouettes creates one of Ayutthaya's most striking images.

Late morning, head to Wat Yai Chai Mongkhon outside the center. Its main chedi rises 236 feet, dominating the plain. Around it, some fifty seated Buddhas wrapped in saffron cloth form an impressive row. This is one of Ayutthaya's best-preserved temples and a powerful image of Thai devotion.

activities.text: In the afternoon, take time to wander Ayutthaya Floating Market (east of the island). It's more recent than traditional markets but offers a fine showcase of local cuisine: boat noodles (kuay tiao reua), khao mok kai (Thai version of biryani), and giant river prawns. Order from the quays.

visits.text: Phra Prang Sam Yot is a 13th-century Khmer temple - three prangs connected by a corridor, built during the reign of King Jayavarman VII. What distinguishes it: hundreds of crab-eating macaques have claimed it as their domain. They climb sculptures, leap from prang to prang, stare directly into your eyes. Stow all shiny objects - glasses, earrings - before entering. Don't stare too long at them. The temple's image appears on the Lopburi provincial flag.

Walk to King Narai's Palace - a vast 17th-century complex built by the sovereign who opened Thailand to European merchants. French, Dutch, and English ambassadors were received here. The national museum installed in the former royal granary displays Cham and Khmer sculptures, reconstructions of court life, and artifacts from local excavations.

activities.text: Get lost in Lopburi's alleyways. It's a city where monkeys have literally taken over: they run along electrical wires, enter grocery stores, jump on motorcycles. Locals have coexisted with them for generations. Watch local-macaque interactions - it's one of Thailand's most surreal spectacles.

map_keywords: Bridge on the River Kwai Kanchanaburi

visits.text: Walk across the Bridge on the River Kwai. This steel bridge built in 1943 by the Japanese army forms part of the infamous Death Railway - 258 miles from Thailand into Burma, constructed in 17 months at the cost of 100,000 deaths including 15,000 Allied prisoners. The angular central spans were rebuilt after Allied bombing in 1945. A passenger train still crosses it twice daily at walking speed.

Head to the JEATH War Museum, 5 minutes' walk from the bridge. JEATH is an acronym for Japan, England, Australia, Thailand, Holland - the nationalities involved in building the railway. This small museum founded in 1977 by a Thai monk displays artifacts, prisoner tools, period photographs, and reconstructions of bamboo barracks. Its stark presentation makes the suffering more tangible than any modern museum could.

Walk to Kanchanaburi War Cemetery (Don Rak). Beneath flowering frangipani trees, 6,982 perfectly aligned headstones mark the graves of Allied prisoners who died on the construction site. The inscriptions - ages, regiments, words from their families - give human reality to the numbers.

activities.text: Late afternoon, walk or cycle along the Kwai River. The town is pleasant in the evening - waterfront terraces on stilts, fresh fish restaurants, quiet bars. Tour operators offer longtail boat rides on the river, between karsts and forests.

visits.text: Take the bus from Kanchanaburi terminal to reach Erawan National Park (90-minute drive). This 212-square-mile park takes its name from the mythological three-headed elephant of Hindu cosmology. Its waterfall unfolds in 7 successive tiers. At tier 2, the turquoise basin is deep and swimmable - small fish will nibble your dead skin, a completely free fish spa. From tier 4 onward, passages steepen. Tier 7 is the reward: a taller cascade in a setting enclosed by limestone cliffs.

activities.text: Swim in the emerald pools at tiers 2 and 3. Arrive early (opens at 8 AM) to avoid weekend crowds. After tier 2, food and drinks are prohibited - eat before ascending. Bring a life jacket (rental on-site) if entering deep pools.

visits.text: Take the train from Kanchanaburi (departure around 6 AM, 100 baht for foreigners). After the bridge over the River Kwai, the line enters the forest and hugs limestone cliffs. Get off at Tham Krasae stop. The 1,475-foot wooden viaduct clings to the cliff face above the Khwae Noi River - you can walk across it. A cave temple nearby sheltered sick prisoners of war.

From Nam Tok, take a songthaew to Hellfire Pass Memorial Museum (50 miles northwest of Kanchanaburi). This site managed by the Australian government traces the construction of the railway's deadliest section - a 246-foot cutting through solid rock, hand-carved. At night, by torchlight, prisoners became shadows in the flames - hence the name 'Hellfire Pass'. The museum is free, understated, and gripping.

activities.text: Walk the Hellfire Pass trail - nearly a mile through the rock cutting. You touch the tool marks still visible on the walls. The place is silent. Panels recount survivors' testimonies. Take your time.

visits.text: Start with Tham Khao Luang Cave, 2.5 miles north of town. Descend rock steps: a 98-foot-high chamber opens before you. Two openings in the pierced vault drop columns of natural light directly onto the 170 Buddha statues populating the 3 chambers. Rama V himself came here to meditate as a young monk. The morning light show is the most powerful.

Next, climb to Phra Nakhon Khiri Historical Park - the summer palace of King Mongkut (Rama IV), completed in 1860 on a 302-foot hill overlooking the city. A funicular provides summit access (40 baht round-trip). On three successive crests: the white chedi of Phra That Chom Phet, the former royal apartments transformed into a museum, and the royal Wat Phra Kaeo. Panoramic 360° view across the Phetchaburi plain.

activities.text: Phetchaburi is renowned for its khanom - traditional Thai desserts. While walking the old town, seek out shops selling roti sai mai, a sweet confection of cotton candy threads wrapped in a thin crepe, and coconut and palm sugar candies. The evening market begins around 5 PM on Phanitchatham Street.

visits.text: Head to Mrigadayavan Palace, 7.5 miles north of Hua Hin. Built in 1924 by Italian architect Ercole Manfredi for King Rama VI, this summer palace is entirely golden teak on stilts, connected by 3,280 feet of aerial walkways. Eighteen pavilions face the sea - the sea breeze circulates beneath the wooden floors. Walk barefoot on the walkways and gaze at the Gulf of Thailand horizon.

Late afternoon, climb Khao Takiab hill south of the beach. A large golden Buddha faces the sea from the summit. Macaques occupy the stairways - stay alert with your belongings. The view over Hua Hin bay and the long sand strip is one of the coast's finest.

activities.text: In the evening, descend to Hua Hin Night Market, intersection of Dechanuchit and Phetkasem Road. After 6:30 PM, seafood stalls invade the street - giant prawns, lobsters, whole fish on charcoal. Grill smoke, mango stall colors, vendors calling out - it's the bustle of popular Thailand, Gulf coast version.

visits.text: Arrive in Amphawa in early afternoon. The floating market comes alive from 2 PM but reveals its true character from 5 PM onward, when lamps illuminate the canal's wooden houses. Cooking boats offer pad thai, grilled prawns, mango sticky rice from the water. You order from the quay, boats dock to deliver.

By boat, reach Wat Bang Kung - an 18th-century military temple whose main hall is literally consumed by the roots of a giant banyan tree. The roots have invaded the walls for two centuries, encircling Buddhas and columns in an embrace of stone and wood.

activities.text: After 8 PM, board a nighttime boat ride on the Mae Klong River to observe fireflies. Hundreds of insects synchronize to flash together in trees along the banks - a rare natural phenomenon. The ride lasts about one hour.

visits.text: Before returning to Bangkok, detour through Maeklong Railway Market, 15 minutes from Amphawa. This market has existed since 1905 on the tracks of a still-active train line. When the siren sounds, vendors fold their awnings within seconds, pull back their stalls of vegetables, fruits, and fish - and the train passes within inches of the merchandise. The ballet resumes after it passes. Trains arrive at 8:30 AM, 11:10 AM, 2:30 PM, and 5:40 PM.

activities.text: Return to Bangkok late morning. Use these final hours for a last stroll through an unexplored neighborhood - Sathorn for colonial architecture, Si Lom for office atmosphere, or the galleries of Soi 51 in Sukhumvit if you missed the creative district.

seo.h1_title: Central Thailand: Bangkok, Ancient Kingdoms & the River Kwai
seo.intro_paragraph: This 14-day circuit traverses the historic heart of Thailand, from Bangkok to the ruins of Ayutthaya (capital for 417 years), passing through Kanchanaburi and its infamous Death Railway. You swim in the turquoise pools of Erawan Falls, sleep at the feet of millennial prangs, and finish with the country's most authentic floating markets at Amphawa.
seo.seo_hook: Gilded Bangkok, UNESCO-listed Ayutthaya ruins, Bridge on the River Kwai, and Erawan cascades in 14 days.
featured_list: Bangkok: Grand Palace, 151-foot Reclining Buddha at Wat Pho, and Chatuchak Market with 15,000 stalls
Ayutthaya: Buddha head trapped in banyan tree roots and three royal chedis at Wat Phra Si Sanphet
Kanchanaburi: Tham Krasae cliff viaduct and 7-tier turquoise Erawan cascades
Amphawa: sunset floating market and nighttime firefly tour on the Mae Klong River

places_taglines.TH::bangkok: Where golden spires pierce the sky and the Chao Phraya carries centuries of stories.
places_taglines.TH::ayutthaya: Headless Buddhas and crumbling prangs guard the memory of Siam's vanished glory.
places_taglines.TH::lopburi: Where macaques rule ancient Khmer temples and local life unfolds amid simian chaos.
places_taglines.TH::kanchanaburi: Where history carved a railway through limestone and waterfalls heal old wounds.
places_taglines.TH::phetchaburi: Sunlight through cave vaults illuminates Buddha rows beneath a king's hilltop palace.
places_taglines.TH::hua-hin: Royal teak pavilions float above turquoise waters where Thai kings once summered.
places_taglines.TH::amphawa: Boats laden with grilled prawns glide past wooden houses as fireflies dance in the darkness.

highlights: Wat Pho houses the 151-foot Reclining Buddha and the Thai massage school operating since 1962
Ayutthaya Historical Park spans 714 acres with over 400 temple ruins across the ancient capital
Erawan National Park's 212 square miles contain 7-tier natural swimming pools in turquoise waters
Amphawa opens its floating market only Friday, Saturday, and Sunday starting from 2 PM

itineraries.title: Gulf Islands: Koh Samui, Koh Pha Ngan and Koh Tao
itineraries.subtitle: 12 days of golden temples facing the sea, boat-only beaches and some of the richest underwater life in the Gulf of Thailand.
itineraries.notes: Ferries between the three islands run frequently (Lomprayah and Seatran). The journey from Koh Samui to Koh Pha Ngan takes 30 minutes, Koh Pha Ngan to Koh Tao around 1h30. The dry season runs from December to April - the best time for diving (visibility 20-30 m). The windy season (June-August) can disrupt crossings and underwater visibility. Ang Thong Marine Park is closed from November to mid-December in some years.
specialties: Tom kha kai with coconut milk

map_keywords: Wat Phra Yai Big Buddha Koh Samui

visits.text: Cross the causeway that connects Koh Samui to the islet of Koh Faan. At the end stands the Big Buddha - 12 meters tall, golden, seated in the Mara posture - staring back at you since 1972. It's often the first thing you spot when landing in Samui. Climb the bell-lined staircase. At the top, views sweep across the entire Bang Rak bay and planes taking off 3 km away.

Two kilometers from Big Buddha, push on to Wat Plai Laem. This modern temple built on an artificial lake blends Chinese and Thai styles. The 18-armed white statue of Guanyin reflects in the water. Catfish in the lake fight over pellets visitors toss them. It's more kitsch than solemn - and that's precisely its charm.

activities.text: Late afternoon, head down to Bophut's Fisherman's Village. This converted fishing quarter lines wooden Chinese shophouses along a calm beach. On Friday nights, the Walking Street comes alive: stalls of grilled fish, smoothies, artisan jewelry. It's the island's most pleasant market - more intimate than Chaweng, without the blasting music.

map_keywords: Emerald Lake Mae Koh Ang Thong

visits.text: Board at 7:30 am from Nathon pier for Ang Thong Marine Park - 42 limestone islands set on an emerald-green sea, 30 km northwest of Samui. The boat docks at Wua Talap, the main island. Climb the 500 steps up to the viewpoint: below, the archipelago spreads like a scale model - jungle-covered islets, turquoise lagoons, deserted white beaches.

Take the boat to Mae Koh island. A 400-meter trail climbs through jungle to the Thale Nai - the Emerald Lake. This inland saltwater lagoon, ringed by limestone cliffs, shifts color with the light. In the morning, it's deep green. It's the most famous image of Ang Thong, and it doesn't disappoint.

activities.text: Kayak between Ang Thong's islets. Paddle into sea caves accessible at low tide and along cliffs where sea eagles nest. Most excursions include kayaking, snorkeling and lunch on Wua Talap beach. Return around 5 pm.

practical_info.booking: Book the day before through your accommodation or agencies in Nathon and Chaweng
practical_info.note: Closed in some years from November to mid-December - check beforehand

map_keywords: Wat Khunaram mummified monk Koh Samui

visits.text: Stop by Wat Khunaram in the central hills. Monk Luong Pordaeng died in 1973 in meditation position. His mummified body is displayed in a glass case - seated, sunglasses on, barely altered after 50 years. For Thai Buddhists, it's a sign of extreme virtue. The place is understated and peaceful.

Continue to Na Muang 1 waterfall, accessible in 5 minutes' walk from the parking lot. Water drops 18 meters into a natural pool of purple granite - the color gives the waterfall its name (na muang = purple water). Swimming possible during rainy season when the flow is strong.

If you have a 4x4 or powerful scooter, climb to the Secret Buddha Garden, at 500 meters altitude in the jungle. This sculpture garden was created in 1976 by a farmer, Nim Thongsuk, who spent 20 years erecting Buddha and goddess statues among the trees. The road up is steep, muddy after rain, and not for scooter beginners.

activities.text: End the day at Lamai Beach, Samui's second beach. Calmer and longer than Chaweng, it stretches 4 km. At the south end, the rocks Hin Ta and Hin Yai (grandfather and grandmother) have very explicit anatomical shapes - Thais love posing there for photos. Dinner with your feet in the sand at one of the beach restaurants.

map_keywords: Lipa Noi Beach Koh Samui sunset

visits.text: Give yourself a morning at Chaweng, Samui's main beach - 5 km of white sand, shallow water, coconut palms. It's the island's busiest and makes no apologies: parasols, fruit vendors, music. Swim early morning when the beach is still calm.

In the afternoon, ride to Nathon, the small administrative capital on the west coast. No tourism here - just shopping streets, a fish market, Chinese shophouses and the main ferry port. Order an iced coffee in one of the old waterfront cafés.

activities.text: Sunset at Lipa Noi, the west coast beach. It faces due west, toward the mainland hills. The water is so shallow you walk 100 meters into the sea and still have water at knee level. The late-day colors - orange, pink, purple - are among Samui's finest.

visits.text: Thirty-minute ferry from Samui. Arrival at Thong Sala, Koh Pha Ngan's main port. Rent a scooter at the pier - it's the only practical way to explore the island. Roads are decent on the west coast, more adventurous on the east and north.

Start with Phaeng Noi waterfall, in the island's center, in Than Sadet National Park. A 500-meter trail leads to a waterfall surrounded by dense jungle. Continuing 30 minutes upward, a viewpoint offers views over the entire west coast and distant islands. Entry 100 baht.

activities.text: Settle into Srithanu on the west coast, the island's yoga and wellness quarter. Relaxed vibe, vegetarian cafés, beachfront yoga studios. It's not for everyone, but it's Koh Pha Ngan's alternative face, far from Haad Rin's parties.

map_keywords: Koh Ma sandbar Mae Haad Koh Phangan

visits.text: Leave early for Bottle Beach (Haad Khuat), on the north coast. Two options: a 2.5 km jungle trail from Chaloklum (1h30, steep) or a taxi-boat from Chaloklum (10 min, 100 baht). The beach is a white sand crescent framed by boulders and coconut palms - no road, no cars, just a few wooden bungalows. It's the image you have in mind when you think 'Thai beach'.

Late afternoon, ride to Mae Haad Beach on the northwest coast. At low tide, a sandbar connects Koh Pha Ngan to the small island of Koh Ma. Cross on foot - ankle-deep water - to the island. Decent snorkeling around rocks on the north side. Check tide times before going.

activities.text: Lunch at the fishing village of Chaloklum, at the back of the north bay. Restaurants serve the morning's catch - grilled sea bass, prawns sautéed with basil, tom yum soup. Chaloklum has remained a real fishing port, with colorful boats at anchor and nets drying on the pier.

map_keywords: Thong Nai Pan Beach Koh Phangan

visits.text: Ride toward the east coast to Than Sadet National Park. The eponymous waterfall flows over granite rocks carved by several Rama kings - Rama V came to bathe here in the late 19th century. Descending the rocks to natural pools takes 20 minutes. Continue to Haad Sadet Beach, a wild beach with two bars and deep water for swimming.

Push on to Thong Nai Pan, the northeast's double bay. Two crescent beaches separated by a rocky headland - Thong Nai Pan Noi (the small one) is quieter, Thong Nai Pan Yai (the big one) has more restaurants. Access via a winding road from the island's center makes it less crowded than the west coast.

activities.text: Take a taxi-boat from Haad Rin to Haad Yuan, the hidden beach on the southeast coast. No road: you arrive by boat or via a 2h trek through jungle. A few wooden bungalows, a bar perched on rocks (Eden Bar), and turquoise water. On Saturday nights, Eden Bar hosts a party that runs until Sunday morning - electronic music vibe in the middle of nature.

practical_info.booking: Taxi-boats from Haad Rin pier, negotiate return in advance
practical_info.note: Bring cash and headlamp if returning late via trail

map_keywords: Kuan Yin Chinese Temple Koh Phangan

visits.text: Head down to Haad Rin, the island's southern tip. This peninsula has two beaches: Haad Rin Nok (east side, the Full Moon Party beach) and Rin Nai (west side, sunsets). Outside party periods, Haad Rin Nok is a beautiful fine-sand beach with clear water. Walk to the small lighthouse at the end of Rin Nai for the view.

Stop at the Chinese Kuan Yin temple, on the west coast between Ban Tai and Thong Sala. A large Guanyin statue gazes at the sea from a dragon-adorned terrace. The temple is more recent but photogenic - and almost always deserted.

activities.text: In the evening, dine at Thong Sala's Phantip Night Market. It's the island's real local market - pad thai, chicken skewers, sticky mango rice, fresh fruit juices. Thais eat there between 6 pm and 9 pm. Less photogenic than Fisherman's Village, but more authentic.

visits.text: Ferry from Koh Pha Ngan (1h30). Arrival at Mae Haad port. Settle into Sairee Beach, the main beach - 1.7 km of sand on the west coast. Dive schools line the beach. Koh Tao is the world's most popular place to get your PADI Open Water - prices are among the planet's lowest and conditions ideal for beginners.

Take a longtail from Mae Haad to Koh Nang Yuan, 1 km northwest. Three islets connected by two white sandbars in a Y shape. Climb the steps to the viewpoint: the plunging view over the three sand strips and turquoise water is one of the most famous images of the Gulf of Thailand. Decent snorkeling on the west side. Paid entry, no plastic allowed on the island.

activities.text: Late afternoon, climb to John Suwan Viewpoint at the island's southern tip. The trail starts from Chalok Baan Kao beach and climbs 20 minutes through rocks. At the top, views embrace the entire Chalok bay and Shark Bay below. It's Koh Tao's best sunset spot.

map_keywords: White Rock dive site Koh Tao
Japanese Gardens dive site Koh Tao

visits.text: Dive day. Koh Tao has 26 dive sites around the island. For beginners, Japanese Gardens offers coral gardens at 5-12 meters depth with clownfish, angelfish and stingrays. Twins, two twin pinnacles covered in corals, is perfect for a first dive. Dive schools pick up divers directly from Sairee beach.

In the afternoon, head down to Shark Bay (Thian Og Bay) south of the island. The name comes from blacktip reef sharks patrolling the shallow waters - harmless, between 1 and 2 meters long. You see them from the beach, often late in the day. Easy snorkeling from shore.

activities.text: If you prefer snorkeling to scuba diving, rent mask and fins at Sairee and head along the northwest coast on foot or by boat toward Mango Bay and Hin Wong Bay. Reefs there are close to shore and visibility often excellent. Hin Wong has large underwater boulders colonized by colorful corals.

map_keywords: Chumphon Pinnacle dive site Koh Tao
Sail Rock dive site Gulf of Thailand
Southwest Pinnacle dive site Koh Tao

visits.text: Second day underwater - the advanced sites. Sail Rock, halfway between Koh Tao and Koh Pha Ngan, is considered the Gulf of Thailand's best dive site. A granite pinnacle piercing the surface, with a vertical chimney divers pass through at 18 meters. Schools of barracuda, giant groupers, and in season (March-May), whale sharks. The excursion takes the entire day - breakfast and lunch included.

activities.text: Alternative if Sail Rock is too far or conditions poor: Chumphon Pinnacle, 12 km northwest. Granite pinnacles between 12 and 35 meters depth, carpeted with anemones. It's the statistically most likely spot to encounter a whale shark around Koh Tao. Southwest Pinnacle, 13 km southwest, offers walls of soft corals and schools of barracuda.

visits.text: Explore the east coast by scooter. First stop: Tanote Bay, a bay wedged between granite boulders with a natural diving rock. Water is crystal clear and reefs start 2 meters from shore. Continue to Ao Leuk, a calmer bay with a gentle slope of sand and corals - perfect for a final snorkeling session.

activities.text: Last sunset on Sairee Beach. Beach bars roll out their cushions on the sand and musicians settle in. It's the end-of-trip vibe - feet in the sand, cold beer, the sun dropping into the Gulf. For the return journey, ferries to Chumphon (mainland, 2h) or back via Koh Pha Ngan and Samui.

seo.h1_title: Gulf Islands: Koh Samui, Koh Pha Ngan and Koh Tao
seo.intro_paragraph: Twelve days climbing the three islands of the Gulf of Thailand, south to north. Koh Samui first - golden Big Buddha, Ang Thong archipelago's 42 limestone islands and fishing village markets. Koh Pha Ngan next - boat-access-only Bottle Beach, royal waterfalls and roadless beaches. Koh Tao finally - 26 dive sites, passing whale sharks and coral reefs two meters from shore.
seo.seo_hook: Golden sea-facing Big Buddha, kayaking through 42 limestone islands, roadless beaches and 26 dive sites - the Gulf of Thailand islands in 12 days.
featured_list: Koh Samui: 12-meter Big Buddha, Ang Thong Marine Park's 42 islands and hidden Emerald Lake
Koh Pha Ngan: Bottle Beach accessible by jungle or taxi-boat, royal Than Sadet waterfall
Koh Tao: 26 dive sites including Sail Rock - the Gulf's finest - and seasonal whale sharks
Koh Nang Yuan: three islets connected by a double Y-shaped white sandbar

places_taglines.TH::koh-samui: Where a 12-meter golden Buddha overlooks the bay and 42 limestone islands float offshore.
places_taglines.TH::koh-pha-ngan: Hidden boat-only beaches meet jungle waterfalls carved by Thai kings.
places_taglines.TH::koh-tao: The planet's dive training capital with 26 sites and passing whale sharks in season.

seo_keywords: koh samui koh phangan koh tao

highlights: Ang Thong Marine Park: 42 limestone islands, hidden Emerald Lake and kayaking through sea caves
Bottle Beach in Koh Pha Ngan: accessible only by jungle or taxi-boat, zero cars
Sail Rock: Gulf's best dive site, vertical chimney at 18 m and seasonal whale sharks
Koh Nang Yuan: three islets connected by double white sandbar, panoramic views from summit

itineraries.title: Northern Thailand: Lanna Temples, Mountains and the Golden Triangle
itineraries.subtitle: 12 days on the road through Lanna temples, dizzying mountain passes, and the birthplace of the first Thai kingdom
itineraries.notes: The Mae Hong Son Loop (routes 108 and 1095) is a winding mountain road—expect double the time compared to the distance. Avoid the rainy season (June-October) for the Doi Inthanon and Mae Hong Son sections. The Kew Mae Pan trail at Doi Inthanon is closed from June 1 to October 30.

visits.text: Enter the old city through the square moat and walk up Ratchadamnoen Road to Wat Chedi Luang. The chedi was built starting in 1391 to an original height of 85 meters—an earthquake in 1545 reduced it to about 60 meters, never fully repaired since. Sculpted elephants guard its base. Look for the city pillar—the Lak Mueang planted here in 1800 is believed to protect the city.

Continue on foot for ten minutes west to Wat Phra Singh. This temple, founded in 1345 to house the ashes of King Fu Kham, is home to 700 resident monks. Inside the Viharn Lai Kham, the smallest building in the complex, examine the murals from 1820—they depict daily life in Chiang Mai two centuries ago. The Phra Singh Buddha statue behind them is one of three identical replicas in the country.

activities.text: On Sunday evening, the Walking Street takes over the entire length of Wualai Road: hundreds of street food stalls, weavers, wood carvers. Order khao soi—the red curry and coconut milk soup with crispy noodles is the northern specialty.

map_keywords: Wat Phra That Doi Suthep Chiang Mai

visits.text: Climb the 309 steps of Wat Phra That Doi Suthep, guarded by two seven-headed nagas. At the top, the 24-meter golden chedi gleams in the morning sun—and the view plunges across the entire Chiang Mai plain, 600 meters below. Legend tells that in 1383, a white elephant carried a Buddha relic all the way here, trumpeted three times, then collapsed. The king built the temple on that exact spot. Arrive before 8:30am to have the chedi to yourself.

In the afternoon, turn toward Wat Umong, at the foot of Doi Suthep, in the forest. This 13th-century temple is nicknamed the 'tunnel temple'—Umong means tunnel in Thai. Enter the galleries carved into the brick mound: Buddhist monks still meditate inside. Outside, Buddhist quotations are engraved on plaques suspended from the trees.

activities.text: Book a Thai cooking class at one of the many schools in the Nimmanhaemin neighborhood. Most offer a morning market visit followed by preparation of 4 to 5 traditional northern dishes—khao soi, larb, pad see ew—which you'll eat on site.

map_keywords: Wat Phra That Lampang Luang Ko Kha
Wat Phra Kaew Don Tao Lampang

visits.text: Twenty kilometers south of Lampang, Wat Phra That Lampang Luang sits on an artificial hillock surrounded by a 17th-century fortified wall—the Burmese used it as a stronghold. The Viharn Luang, built in 1476, is considered the oldest standing wooden building in Thailand. The interior pillars are decorated with lai kham, golden motifs on black lacquer. At the back of the viharn, the golden ku houses the Phra Chao Lan Thong Buddha, venerated since the 16th century.

In town, push through the door of Wat Phra Kaew Don Tao on the bank of the Wang River. This temple housed the Emerald Buddha for 32 years (1436-1468) before the statue departed for Chiang Mai and then Bangkok. What remains here is the Phra Kaew Don Tao Buddha in crystal, enshrined in a golden niche of the Mondop. The adjacent chedi dates from the 12th century.

activities.text: Ride in a horse-drawn carriage to tour the old town—the drivers know all the temples in the center. This is the only place in Thailand where this mode of transport is still in tourist operation.

map_keywords: Wat Rong Suea Ten Chiang Rai

visits.text: Thirteen kilometers south of Chiang Rai, Wat Rong Khun—the White Temple—is entirely covered in white plaster inlaid with thousands of mirror fragments. Approach via the Sukavadee Bridge: on each side, hands reaching skyward emerge from the ground, symbols of souls who haven't reached nirvana. Artist Chalermchai Kositpipat funded and built it himself, with work begun in the late 1990s and still ongoing. Inside, the frescoes blend Buddhist scenes with pop culture characters—Batman, Neo from The Matrix. Photos are forbidden in the main building.

The Baan Dam Museum, 10 km north of the city center, is the exact opposite of the White Temple. Forty dark wooden buildings arranged in a garden—the work of national artist Thawan Duchanee. He used animal skins, skulls, buffalo horns. The ensemble explores themes of death and the cycle of life in Buddhist philosophy.

End the day at Wat Rong Suea Ten, the Blue Temple, 6 km north of the center. The main hall is entirely painted in a shade between sapphire and cerulean, with a large white seated Buddha at the back. The name means 'temple of the dancing tiger'—tigers allegedly once leapt through the abandoned ruins.

activities.text: In the evening, wander through Chiang Rai's Night Bazaar, south of the city center. Karen tribe stalls, Hmong embroidery, mountain coffee. Order naem ngiao—the spicy pork and tomato soup, a northern specialty often missing from Chiang Mai menus.

visits.text: Head down to the confluence of the Mekong and Ruak rivers at Sop Ruak. This is where the Thai, Burmese, and Laotian borders meet—the historic Golden Triangle. Thailand eradicated poppy cultivation on its territory as early as the 1970s-80s through royal substitution programs. Across the border in Myanmar, production continued well beyond. A golden Buddha statue stands on a boat-shaped platform anchored at the edge of the Mekong. From the bank, watch Laos to the right and Burma to the left.

Two kilometers north of Sop Ruak, the Hall of Opium traces the history of the drug in the region, from its 19th-century medicinal uses to its eradication. The museum is one of the most modern in the country—allow two hours. It's closed on Mondays.

Back in Chiang Rai, push through the door of Wat Phra Kaew—this is where the Emerald Buddha was discovered in 1434 inside a stucco-covered chedi. Lightning had split the stucco, revealing the green jade. The statue then departed for Lampang, Chiang Mai, and finally Bangkok. A replica remains here.

activities.text: Embark on a short longtail boat trip on the Mekong from Sop Ruak—you'll cruise along the Burmese shore and approach the Laotian island of Don Sao. No visa needed to disembark for a few minutes on the Lao side.

map_keywords: Ang Ka Nature Trail Doi Inthanon

visits.text: First stop climbing toward the summit: Wachirathan Waterfall, whose name means 'diamond stream.' Water plunges 70 meters down a granite escarpment. In good weather, a permanent rainbow forms at the base. This is the most powerful waterfall in the park.

At 2,560 meters altitude, the twin pagodas dominate the surrounding valleys. The first, Naphamethinidon, was erected in 1987 for King Bhumibol's 60th birthday. The second, Naphaphonphumisiri, in 1992 for Queen Sirikit's 60th birthday. Their flowered gardens change color with the seasons—Siamese cherry blossoms from late January to early February.

Continue to Thailand's highest point: 2,565 meters. In January, it averages 6°C here. The Ang Ka trail takes you along a 360-meter boardwalk above a marsh of moss and orchids. This is one of the few places in Thailand where you can spot Himalayan bird species.

activities.text: If you're here between November and March, take the Kew Mae Pan trail—a 3 km loop through conifer forest and highland savanna, with views across the Burmese ridges. Bring a jacket: temperatures can drop to 5°C in the morning.

map_keywords: Wat Phra That Doi Kong Mu Mae Hong Son
Wat Chong Klang Mae Hong Son
Wat Chong Kham Mae Hong Son
Nong Kham Lake Mae Hong Son

visits.text: Descending toward Mae Hong Son from Doi Inthanon, stop at the Doi Mae U Kho viewpoint if you're here between mid-November and mid-December. The hills are then covered with bua tong—wild yellow sunflowers that bloom on the slopes without being cultivated. Several kilometers of bright yellow against a green mountain backdrop. Outside this period, continue without stopping: the road itself is the spectacle.

Climb up to Wat Phra That Doi Kong Mu, perched on the hill overlooking the entire town. Two white Burmese stupas built in 1860 gleam at the summit. Below, Mae Hong Son bathes in a sea of morning mist. The spectacle is most striking at dawn.

At the edge of Nong Kham Lake, the twin temples—Wat Chong Kham (1827) and Wat Chong Klang (1867-71)—reflect their gilded tiered roofs in the water. These are Burmese Shan-style temples, very different from the Lanna wats seen elsewhere. Inside Wat Chong Klang, look for the glass paintings illustrating scenes from the Buddha's life.

activities.text: In the cool season (October-March), a small night market sets up along Nong Kham Lake. Order khao tom—Thai rice soup for the evening—or try grilled river fish. The atmosphere is more local than touristy.

map_keywords: Su Tong Pae Bridge Mae Hong Son

visits.text: Before leaving Mae Hong Son province, stop at the Su Tong Pae Bridge. This 500-meter bamboo bridge built in 2012 by monks and villagers crosses the rice paddies between Mae Hong Son and a neighboring monastery. Early in the morning, Buddhist novices cross it in single file to collect alms—a calm image, without any artifice.

Arrive in Pai and head straight to Pai Canyon, 5 km south of the village. Red-ochre rock formations with sharp ridges—the trails without guardrails run along crests sometimes barely a meter wide. Around 5pm, the sun grazes the walls and everything turns orange.

activities.text: Pai's Walking Street comes alive every evening on the main street. Live musicians, pad thai stands, grilled sai ua sausages, fruit yogurt. Pai blends backpackers, hill tribe villagers, and Thai families—the atmosphere is warmer than cosmopolitan.

visits.text: Rent a scooter and set out early for Mo Paeng Waterfall, 8 km from Pai. It consists of several natural pools with rocky slides. The water is soft and cool, the setting surrounded by jungle. It's noticeably quieter than the attractions in the village center.

On the way back, make a detour to the Land Split—a crack in the earth a few meters wide and a hundred meters long, opened suddenly during a landslide in 2008. It's surrounded by terraced rice paddies. Not essential, but it only takes ten minutes.

In late afternoon, climb up to Yun Lai viewpoint. The sun sets behind the Burmese ridges and the Pai valley fills with golden mist. It's also the best spot at sunrise—come at 5:30am if you're staying in the area.

activities.text: Plunge into the Tha Pai hot springs, 7 km from the center. A cold stream mixes with geothermal springs over rocks in the open air. In the cool season (November-February), it's particularly pleasant. Entry is paid.

visits.text: Long drive south from Pai. Arrive in Sukhothai in late afternoon and enter the historical park directly—it's open until 6pm. This is where the first independent Thai kingdom was founded in 1238, breaking free from the Khmer empire. Its king Ramkhamhaeng invented the Thai alphabet here in 1292. Start with Wat Mahathat, when the light turns low.

Wat Sa Si, a few hundred meters away, sits on a small island connected by a footbridge. A Sri Lankan-style chedi and a standing brick Buddha reflect in the water lilies. This is one of the most photographed spots in the park, especially at sunset.

activities.text: Rent a bicycle at the park entrance—30 to 50 baht per day. The terrain is flat and the paths are paved. It's the most pleasant way to move between the different zones of the site.

visits.text: Leave early for Wat Si Chum, in the northern zone. Approach the narrow slit in the brick mondop's facade: the Phra Achana Buddha—'the one who knows no fear'—stares at you from 15 meters high. Its fingers alone measure 1.3 meters. This is the largest seated stone Buddha in the park. An inscription found here in 1293 is the oldest written document in the Thai alphabet.

Return to the central zone for Wat Si Sawai. Its three Khmer-style prangs—square-based towers—date from the 12th century, even before the kingdom's founding. This was a Hindu sanctuary dedicated to Shiva, transformed into a Buddhist temple during the expansion of Theravada Buddhism.

The Ramkhamhaeng National Museum, just at the entrance to the central zone, displays the 1292 stele with the first Thai alphabet inscriptions, bronze Sukhothai-style Buddhas, and Sawankhalok ceramics. Allow 1.5 hours to tour it properly.

activities.text: Cycle to the temples in the western zone, less visited. Wat Saphan Hin is built on a 200-meter hill—a long stone pathway leads to a tall standing Buddha overlooking the surrounding rice paddies. Practically no one after 4pm.

visits.text: The northern zone also houses Wat Phra Phai Luang—three 12th-century Khmer prangs surrounded by moats. The site is overgrown with vegetation in places and practically deserted most of the time. A very different atmosphere from the restored central zone.

If you have half a day to spare, push on to Si Satchanalai, 50 km north. This was Sukhothai's twin city—just as large, just as well preserved, with ten times fewer visitors. Wat Chang Lom there is surrounded by sculpted elephants in the round. The atmosphere is that of a forgotten archaeological site.

activities.text: Before leaving Sukhothai, buy Sawankhalok ceramics at the old town market. These blue-gray celadon-style pottery pieces, manufactured in the region since the 13th century, are the oldest Thai craft objects.

seo.h1_title: Northern Thailand: Lanna Temples, Mountains and the Golden Triangle
seo.intro_paragraph: Northern Thailand means twelve days between six-century-old Lanna temples, mountain roads with 1,864 curves, and the ruins of the first Thai kingdom. We start with the three major cities—Chiang Mai and its 350 temples, Lampang the city of horse carriages, Chiang Rai and its colorful temples—before diving into the Mae Hong Son Loop, the country's most challenging mountain circuit. We finish at Sukhothai, founded in 1238, birthplace of the Thai alphabet and a UNESCO World Heritage site.
seo.seo_hook: Lanna temples, the Golden Triangle, 1,864 mountain curves, and the cradle of the Thai kingdom in 12 days.
featured_list: Chiang Mai: 309 steps to reach the 24-meter golden chedi of Wat Phra That Doi Suthep
Chiang Rai: the White Temple, entirely covered in mirror fragments, and the sapphire Blue Temple
Mae Hong Son Loop: 1,864 certified mountain curves between Doi Inthanon (2,565 m) and Pai
Sukhothai: the 15-meter Phra Achana Buddha and the first Thai alphabet inscriptions (1292)

places_taglines.TH::chiang-mai: Where ancient Lanna kings built over 350 temples behind fortified moats and gates
places_taglines.TH::lampang: The last city in Thailand where horse-drawn carriages still clatter down the streets
places_taglines.TH::chiang-rai: Where contemporary artists reimagined Buddhist temples in dazzling white, sapphire blue, and midnight black
places_taglines.TH::doi-inthanon: The roof of Thailand at 2,565 meters, wrapped in morning mist and rhododendron forests
places_taglines.TH::mae-hong-son: A valley town floating in clouds, where Shan-style temples mirror themselves in the morning lake
places_taglines.TH::pai: Where bamboo bridges cross rice paddies and canyon walls turn orange at golden hour
places_taglines.TH::sukhothai: The birthplace of Thai civilization, where the alphabet was born and lotus ponds reflect 700-year-old Buddhas

highlights: 309 steps to reach the 24-meter golden chedi of Doi Suthep, perched at 1,073 meters
1,864 certified curves on the Mae Hong Son Loop, the country's most winding mountain road
Wat Si Chum at Sukhothai: a 15-meter Buddha glimpsed through a narrow slit in the brickwork
Golden Triangle: the confluence of the Mekong and Ruak rivers, where Thailand, Laos, and Burma meet

itineraries.title: Southern Thailand: Andaman Coast, Karsts & Offshore Islands
itineraries.subtitle: From Hua Hin to the Malaysian border: 14 days along the Andaman coast — from Khao Sok's jungle to Koh Lipe's crystal waters.
itineraries.notes: This itinerary runs entirely along the Andaman (western) coast. The provinces of Yala, Pattani and Narathiwat (Deep South) are excluded — not recommended for travel due to government advisories. The Similan Islands are accessible only between November and April. Koh Lipe is closed to visitors from May through October. Factor in ferries from Krabi to Koh Lanta and from Pak Bara to Koh Lipe.

visits.text: Head down to Hat Thung Wua Laen, the finest beach along Chumphon's coastline. This 2.7-kilometer ribbon of white sand runs alongside a quiet road lined with bungalows and modest seafood restaurants. The name means 'beach where the buffalo runs' — a remnant from when herds grazed here. On weekdays, you'll practically have it to yourself.

Climb up to Khao Matsee Viewpoint late in the day. The overlook commands the coastline and the marine park islands offshore. A terrace café waits at the summit — the perfect spot to watch the sun sink over the Gulf of Thailand.

activities.text: Drive from Hua Hin or Bangkok to Chumphon (3.5 hours from Hua Hin, around 500 km from Bangkok). Chumphon is also a stop on the Bangkok–Surat Thani overnight train — arrival possible in the early morning.

In the evening, explore Chumphon's night market, set up in the town center. The stalls selling tropical fruit from local orchards are particularly abundant — mangosteens, durians, rambutan depending on the season.

visits.text: Soak your feet at Raksawarin Hot Springs, 2 km from Ranong's center. Water gushes out at 65°C year-round — three separate pools (the Father, the Mother, the Child) let you choose your intensity. King Rama V visited here in 1890 and named the access road 'Chon Ra-u,' meaning 'hot water.' The public pools are free; private enclosures are available for a small fee.

Stroll along Saphan Pla fishing port, one of the busiest along the Andaman coast. Across the Kraburi River, you can see Kawthaung — Myanmar's southernmost continental point — just a few hundred meters away. Colorful longboats shuttle between the two countries.

activities.text: Coastal drive from Chumphon to Ranong (around 2 hours) via Highway 4, which follows a mountainous, heavily forested shoreline. Ranong is Thailand's rainiest province — 8 months of monsoon — but this gives it vegetation of remarkable density.

In the evening, take a turn through the night market on Kamlangsap Road, 200 m from Highway 4. Small but lively, it concentrates the Thai-Burmese specialties of the region — strong Hokkien influence in the snacks and soups.

visits.text: Check into a riverside bungalow at the entrance to the national park. The forest surrounding you is estimated to be over 160 million years old — older and richer in biodiversity than the Amazon. As you make your way down to your lodging, listen closely: male gibbons unleash their territorial calls at dawn.

Set off hiking the park trails late in the afternoon, when light filters through the canopy. The park is home to wild elephants, Malayan sun bears, tapirs, and clouded leopards. Between January and March, the rafflesia can bloom here — its flower reaches up to 80 cm in diameter, one of the largest in the world.

activities.text: Drive from Ranong to Khao Sok (around 3 hours) via Highway 4 then Route 401. Along the way, you'll pass through rubber and palm oil plantations that blanket the southern hills. Arrive in the afternoon to enjoy the forest light.
Night safari on the park trails with a local guide. The forest reveals itself most at night: slow lorises, palm civets, frogs of improbable size. Bring a headlamp and closed shoes.

map_keywords: Cheow Lan Lake Ratchaprapha Dam Khao Sok
Khao Sam Kloe Guilin Cheow Lan

visits.text: Board a longtail boat onto Cheow Lan Lake from the Ratchaprapha pier. The lake, created by the dam of the same name in 1986, spans 165 km² deep in the forest. The limestone karsts — some rising to 960 meters — plunge straight into the emerald water. First stop: Khao Sam Kloe, a trio of limestone towers surging from the water, which Thais nickname 'the Guilin of Cheow Lan.'
Explore Phra Kay Petch cave, 100 meters long, carved into the Klong Ka tributary. Inside, colored stalactites and stalagmites drop into the darkness. Millions of bats nest on the ceiling.

activities.text: Full-day longtail excursion on Cheow Lan Lake from Ratchaprapha pier. Book the day before through your accommodation. Leave early — morning mist shrouds the karsts and the light is perfect before 9 a.m.

Kayak from the floating bungalows: paddle among the limestone islets in absolute calm. Most accommodations on the water provide kayaks to their guests. Early morning, watch for monkeys in the treetops along the shores.

map_keywords: Tsunami Police Boat Memorial Khao Lak

visits.text: Visit the tsunami memorial on Highway 4, north of Khao Lak. A police boat — number 813 — rests there, carried 2 km inland by the wave of December 26, 2004. This boat belonged to the Royal Thai Navy and was at sea that morning. Khao Lak recorded the highest run-up wave ever measured in Thailand that day.

Walk along Nang Thong Beach and Bang Niang Beach, Khao Lak's two main beaches. A post-tsunami regulation bans high-rise construction along the shoreline — result: no towers, no concrete dominating the sea, just discreet resorts in the trees. Khao Lak Beach stretches 16 km in total.

activities.text: Drive Khao Sok to Khao Lak (around 1.5 hours). Khao Lak is the departure point for excursions to the Similan Islands, accessible only from October to May from Thap Lamu pier. Off-season, inquire about snorkeling trips around the coastal islands.

If you're here between November and April, book a snorkeling trip to the Similan Islands departing from Thap Lamu pier (60 km offshore). The Similans rank among Asia's top ten dive sites — multicolored corals, manta rays, white-tip reef sharks. The park closes from May to October.

practical_info.distance: 60 km offshore, departing from Thap Lamu pier

visits.text: Get lost in Phuket's old town, around Thalang Road and Soi Romanee. The Sino-Portuguese shophouses with pastel facades date from the early 20th-century mining boom, when Hokkien merchants and Portuguese engineers fused their styles. Soi Romanee was once Phuket's entertainment district — today, its pink houses shelter cafés and boutiques. It's the most photographed street on the island.

Step into the Thai Hua Museum on Krabi Road, housed in a former Sino-Portuguese school from 1934. The exhibition traces the history of Phuket's Baba-Nyonya (Peranakan) community — born from the crossroads of Hokkien Chinese miners and local Thai women, with their own cuisine, language, and crafts.

On Sundays from 4 p.m. to 10 p.m., Thalang Road transforms into a pedestrian market. Street food stalls, local musicians, and artisans take over the street: it's the best time to sample Phuket's Sino-Thai specialties in the open air.

activities.text: Drive Khao Lak to Phuket (1.5 hours). Stay in the old town rather than Patong — you'll sleep in a heritage building, 10 minutes' walk from everything that matters.

Go hunting for the street art scattered around the old town since 2014, when Phuket and Georgetown (Penang) became sister cities. Look for Alex Face's work on Phang Nga Road and Rukkij Kuanhavej's murals at the corner of Soi Romanee.

map_keywords: James Bond Island Phang Nga Bay
Koh Panyee Floating Village Phang Nga

visits.text: Join a longtail tour of Phang Nga Bay from Tha Dan pier. First stop: Koh Tapu, that 20-meter rock jutting vertically from the sea — made famous as 'James Bond Island' after filming 'The Man with the Golden Gun' in 1974. The real name of the neighboring island, Khao Phing Kan, means 'mountains leaning against each other' — a massive split limestone block leans against its twin.

Stop at Koh Panyee, a floating village founded by Muslim fishermen over 200 years ago. All the houses, the school, the mosque, and even a football pitch are perched on stilts against a vertical limestone tower. Walk the wooden walkways — away from the main tourist flow, a few alleys still show the village's daily life.

activities.text: Full-day longtail excursion in Phang Nga Bay. Depart from Tha Dan Pier (Phang Nga Town) — the boat ride is shorter and you'll cross paths with fewer speedboats. Opt for a private longtail for more flexibility.

practical_info.booking: Leave before 9 a.m. to avoid the crowds on Koh Tapu

activities.text: Kayak through the 'hongs' — those marine caverns carved into limestone cliffs by erosion, accessible only at low tide by kayak. Inside, a miniature jungle and a secret lagoon await. Operators based in Phang Nga provide paddling guides.

visits.text: Take a longtail from Ao Nang to Railay — 15 minutes across turquoise water between cliffs collapsing into the sea. Railay is a peninsula completely cut off from the road by its own karsts: you can only reach it by boat. Walk to Phra Nang Cave Beach, at the end of the coastal path. The cliffs lean over the white sand, creating an almost surreal scene.

Visit Phra Nang Cave (Princess Cave) at the far end of the beach. Local fishermen have left carved wooden ex-votos here for decades — a tradition linked to the legend of a princess drowned at sea. The cave is a sacred place where the devotion of local sailors can be read in every offering.

activities.text: Drive Phuket to Krabi (around 3 hours). Arrive early afternoon to catch the longtail from Ao Nang to Railay. Boats leave as soon as they're full — 8 passengers minimum. Otherwise share the cost with other travelers.
Rock climbing on Railay West's limestone cliffs: one of Southeast Asia's premier limestone climbing sites. Operators offer sessions for beginners and advanced climbers alike, gear provided.

practical_info.note: Multiple levels available, beginner to expert

map_keywords: Tiger Cave Temple Krabi Wat Tham Suea

visits.text: Tackle Wat Tham Suea (Tiger Cave Temple) early in the morning, before the heat sets in. The temple was founded in 1975 by a monk meditating in the cave — tiger paw prints are still visible in the rock. The ascent counts 1,260 steps to the summit at 309 meters altitude. At the top: a golden Buddha, a chedi, and a panoramic view over Krabi, the Andaman Sea, and the 836 islands of the province. Allow 45 minutes to 1 hour to climb. Bring water.

activities.text: 4-island longtail excursion from Ao Nang: Koh Poda, Tup Island (sandbar submerged at high tide), Chicken Island, and Koh Mor. Snorkeling stop between the islands. Lunch on the beach before returning late afternoon.

practical_info.booking: Book early morning in Ao Nang

map_keywords: Mu Ko Lanta National Park Lighthouse

visits.text: Arrive at Koh Lanta by ferry from Krabi. Get settled, then head down to Lanta Old Town, on the island's east coast. These are century-old teak houses on stilts, once a Chinese trading post on the maritime route between Phuket, Penang, and Singapore. The inhabitants: Thai-Chinese, Thai-Muslims, and Chao Lay (sea nomads). Lunch at one of the wooden restaurants whose tables overlook the sea.

Drive down to the southern tip of the island to enter Mu Ko Lanta National Park. Short hike to the lighthouse — it stands at the end of a headland plunging into the Andaman Sea. Around you, dense jungle and reefs visible through the transparent water.

activities.text: Ferry Krabi to Koh Lanta from Ao Nang or Krabi Town pier. The crossing takes around 2 hours depending on the boat type. Upon arrival, rent a scooter in Saladan — it's the best way to explore the island at your own pace.
Sunset from Kantiang Bay, on the island's south. This long, lightly frequented bay offers Koh Lanta's most peaceful panorama — a few terraced resorts and a nearly deserted beach in low season.

map_keywords: Koh Rok Nok snorkeling Koh Lanta

visits.text: Take a longtail to Koh Rok, 47 km south of Koh Lanta. This pair of uninhabited islands in the Mu Ko Lanta marine park is one of the region's finest snorkeling spots: water with 20–25 meter visibility, corals in excellent condition, sea turtles regularly sighted.

activities.text: Snorkeling around Koh Rok: gear up from the boat and explore the reefs in open water. Operators based on Koh Lanta organize small-group trips — departure 8:30 a.m., return around 4 p.m.

practical_info.note: November–April only — the island is closed during monsoon season
practical_info.booking: Book the day before through your accommodation

activities.text: In the evening, return to Long Beach or Klong Dao Beach for sunset. Koh Lanta faces due west — sunsets over the Andaman Sea are among the most beautiful on the Thai coast.

map_keywords: Tham Le Khao Kob Cave Trang

visits.text: Detour to Tham Le Khao Kob cave, 35 km north of Trang. You visit it by rowboat on an underground river that runs through a limestone massif. The boatmen know every inch of the vault — at certain passages, you have to lie down in the boat to avoid hitting the ceiling. Locals call the narrowest section 'the dragon's backbone.'

In the evening, explore Trang's night market, known as 'Centrepoint,' a few streets east of the train station. Trang is renowned for its dim sum and charcoal-roasted pork — a legacy of Hokkien immigrants who populated the town when the tin mines were running full tilt. Vendors offer 20 varieties of curry at once. Order by pointing.

activities.text: Return ferry to the mainland from Koh Lanta (1.5–2 hours via Trang or Pak Meng pier). From the pier, drive to Trang (30–45 min). Trang is an ordinary Thai town with extraordinary gastronomy.

Roti mataba for breakfast at one of the halal stalls near the station: pancake stuffed with spiced beef, onion, and egg, served with yellow curry for dipping. It's Trang's signature morning specialty that everyone orders standing up before heading out.

visits.text: Disembark at Pattaya Beach from the speedboat arriving from Pak Bara. Koh Lipe is Thailand's southernmost island — 30 km from Langkawi, Malaysia. It measures barely 4 km² and can be explored entirely on foot. Cross the Walking Street, the pedestrian artery linking Pattaya Beach to Sunrise Beach in 15 minutes: swimsuit shops, beachfront restaurants, fruit vendors, masseurs. Laid-back vibe, no cars.

Settle in on Sunrise Beach (Hat Chao Ley) late afternoon. It's the island regulars' favorite beach — finer sand, calmer water, views of the small islets of Koh Kra and Koh Usen. Opposite, the larger Koh Adang closes the horizon to the north.

activities.text: Speedboat from Pak Bara Pier to Koh Lipe (1.5 hours). Pak Bara is 90 km south of Trang by road. Arrive at the pier early in the morning — boats generally depart between 9 a.m. and 11:30 a.m. From Koh Lipe, a ferry also connects Langkawi (Malaysia) directly.

practical_info.booking: Boats depart mornings only — arrive early at Pak Bara pier

activities.text: Snorkeling from Sunrise Beach: two islets (Koh Kra and Koh Usen) are reachable by kayak in 20 minutes. Corals, clownfish, and giant clams at just a few meters depth. Rent mask and fins directly on the beach.

map_keywords: Koh Adang viewpoint Tarutao National Park

visits.text: Take the taxi-boat from Sunrise Beach to Koh Adang (10 minutes). The island is jungle-covered and nearly deserted. Three viewpoints succeed each other climbing the trail: the first is reached in 15 minutes, the last — Chado Hill — in 45 to 60 minutes. From the summit, Koh Lipe appears in its entirety, ringed by turquoise reefs. It's the journey's final panorama.
Last swim at Sunset Beach, a 15-minute walk west of Walking Street. The beach faces the Malaysian horizon. A few rocks on the south side harbor corals accessible by swimming at high tide.

activities.text: Kayak around the small islets north of Sunrise Beach: Koh Kra, Koh Usen, Koh Kla. Allow 3 hours to circle them completely. The water is so clear you'll paddle above the corals without having to dive. Rental operators are right on Sunrise Beach.

For the return: speedboat to Pak Bara then road to Hat Yai (domestic airport) or Trang (train station). From Koh Lipe, it's also possible to reach Langkawi in Malaysia directly by ferry to continue the journey south.

seo.h1_title: Southern Thailand: Andaman Coast, Karsts & Offshore Islands
seo.intro_paragraph: From Hua Hin to Koh Lipe, this itinerary traces the Andaman coast from end to end — passing through the jungle of Khao Sok with its floating bungalows on a lake ringed by 160-million-year-old karsts, Phuket's Sino-Portuguese old town, the limestone cliffs of Railay accessible only by boat, and the pristine reefs of Koh Lipe, 30 km from Malaysia. Fourteen days between primary rainforest and turquoise waters, never retracing your steps.
seo.seo_hook: Jungle 160 million years old, cliffs plunging into an emerald sea, roadless islands on the Malaysian border — southern Thailand's Andaman side, end to end.
featured_list: Khao Sok: Cheow Lan Lake ringed by 100 limestone towers and floating bungalows in the heart of the park
Phuket Old Town: 19th-century Sino-Portuguese shophouses, Baba-Nyonya street art, and night markets
Railay: roadless peninsula accessible only by longtail, 300-meter cliffs above white sand
Koh Lipe: Thailand's southernmost island, coral reefs with 20 m visibility and sunsets facing Langkawi

places_taglines.TH::chumphon: First stop down south: white sand beaches and the gateway to the Gulf of Thailand islands.
places_taglines.TH::ranong: Hot springs at 65°C, Myanmar border crossing, and the country's densest rainforest.
places_taglines.TH::khao-sok: Rainforest 160 million years in the making, Cheow Lan Lake, and floating bungalows among karsts.
places_taglines.TH::khao-lak: Long, quiet beaches, 2004 tsunami memorial, and departure point for the Similan Islands.
places_taglines.TH::phuket: Sino-Portuguese old town, Baba-Nyonya street art, and night markets among pastel shophouses.
places_taglines.TH::krabi: Railay cut off by road, Tiger Cave Temple's 1,260 steps, and 4-island boat tours.
places_taglines.TH::koh-lanta: Stilt houses of Lanta Old Town, calm southern beaches, and snorkeling at Koh Rok.
places_taglines.TH::trang: Sino-Thai town famed for dim sum, charcoal-roasted pork, and underground river caves.
places_taglines.TH::koh-lipe: Thailand's southernmost island: crystal reefs, Walking Street, and 30 km from Langkawi.

highlights: Cheow Lan Lake in Khao Sok: 165 km² surrounded by 100 limestone towers in southern Thailand's largest primary rainforest, estimated at over 160 million years old.
Railay Beach: peninsula accessible only by longtail, with Phra Nang Cave Beach and its cliffs towering directly above white sand.
Koh Lipe: Thailand's southernmost island, 30 km from Langkawi, waters with 20–25 m visibility in Tarutao marine park.
Phuket Old Town: Sino-Portuguese quarter with 19th-century shophouses, Baba-Nyonya heritage, and street art from the twinning with Penang.

itineraries.title: Essential Thailand: Temples, Jungle & Beaches in 2 Weeks
itineraries.subtitle: From Bangkok to the Andaman Islands via Chiang Mai and Chiang Rai: the complete Thailand circuit for first-time visitors.
itineraries.notes: This itinerary relies on three domestic flights (BKK → CNX, CEI → BKK, BKK → KBV) and two ferry crossings (Krabi → Koh Phi Phi and Koh Phi Phi → Phuket). Maya Bay is closed in August and September - verify access dates before booking.

visits.text: Enter the Grand Palace through the main gate on Na Phra Lan Road. The complex sprawls across 218,400 m² - a city within the city. Built in 1782 by King Rama I using bricks salvaged from the ruins of Ayutthaya, it comprises over 100 buildings with golden roofs and multicolored mosaics. Inside Wat Phra Kaew, look for the Emerald Buddha statue: 66 cm carved from a single block of solid jade. Only the king may change its golden costumes - three times a year, according to the season. Allow at least 2.5 hours. Dress code enforced: shoulders and knees covered. Beware of strangers telling you the palace is closed - it's the neighborhood's main tourist scam.

Walk 700 m south to Wat Pho. The Reclining Buddha is there: 46 m long, 15 m high, covered in gold leaf, so large it barely fits inside its building. Look at the soles of his feet, inlaid with mother-of-pearl in 108 patterns representing the auspicious signs of the Buddha. The temple is also the official birthplace of Thai massage - a school has trained practitioners here since 1962. Before leaving, treat yourself to a foot massage session in the courtyard.

activities.text: Take the ferry from Tha Tien Pier (3 baht) to cross the Chao Phraya. Wat Arun appears on the opposite bank, its 82 m central prang covered in porcelain fragments - pieces of Chinese plates used as ballast on trading junks. Climb the steep prang steps to see the mosaics up close and enjoy the view of the Grand Palace across the river. Stay for late afternoon light - the tower catches fire before sunset.

visits.text: Dive into Chinatown by walking up Yaowarat Road. This neighborhood has existed since 1782: King Rama I relocated the Chinese community here to clear space for the Grand Palace. Today it's a succession of gold shops, traditional medicine pharmacies and alleyways that smell of roast duck. Push open the door of Wat Mangkon Kamalawat, a Buddhist-Taoist-Confucian temple tucked in the heart of the quarter - worshippers burn incense at all hours. At night, the sidewalks become open-air kitchens: hoy tod (oyster omelet), kuay jab (rolled noodles with crispy pork), bean paste desserts. Arrive after 6 PM for the neighborhood at full throttle.

The Jim Thompson House is an unexpected museum: six 17th-century teak houses dismantled and reconstructed in 1959 by an American businessman who revived Thailand's silk industry after World War II. His collection - paintings, celadons, statues - sits exactly where he left it in 1967, when he disappeared during a walk in Malaysia and was never found. The guided tour lasts 45 minutes. The shaded gardens offer refuge from Bangkok's noise.

activities.text: Board a longtail boat from Tha Chang Pier to explore Thonburi's klongs. On the Chao Phraya's west bank, the city changes centuries: wooden houses on stilts, orchid vendors, temples hidden behind vegetation. Operators offer 1 to 2-hour tours through secondary canals where cars don't exist. This is Bangkok before the skyscrapers.

visits.text: If you're here on a Saturday or Sunday, Chatuchak is a must: 15,000 stalls across 35 hectares - the world's largest weekend market. Handicrafts, ceramics, silks, plants, vinyl, clothing: each aisle is a different neighborhood. Arrive before 10 AM - after that, heat and crowds make wandering difficult. To navigate, photograph the entrance map: sections are numbered 1 to 27.

In the afternoon, head down to Charoen Krung, Bangkok's creative quarter. This is the old 19th and 20th-century trade route - European-style buildings sit alongside artist workshops, design galleries and cafés in renovated shophouses. The TCDC (Thailand Creative & Design Centre) deserves 45 minutes: exhibitions of Thai design and a library open to all. Evening falls slowly over the river from the terraces of Talat Noi.

activities.text: Evening flight from Bangkok to Chiang Mai (Suvarnabhumi or Don Mueang airport, 1h20 flight). Book early - evening flights fill up fast. Upon arrival, settle in and scout the old city: you start tomorrow morning.

visits.text: Begin with Wat Chedi Luang in the old city center. Construction started in 1391 to house the ashes of King Saen Muang Ma's father, completed in 1475 at 82 m high - the tallest structure in the Lanna kingdom. In 1545, an earthquake claimed the top 30 meters. The truncated structure has stood ever since. In the eastern niche, a replica of the Emerald Buddha recalls that the original stayed here a century before being transferred to Bangkok. Four staircases guarded by nagas - mythical serpents - frame the four faces. Allow 45 minutes to 1 hour.

Ten minutes' walk west, Wat Phra Singh is Chiang Mai's most revered temple. Founded in 1345 by King Phayu, it houses the Phra Buddha Sihing statue - its origin remains a mystery, with some chronicles tracing it from Sri Lanka via India and Ayutthaya. Enter the Viharn Lai Kham: 19th-century murals depict daily life under the Lanna kingdom. Each April during Songkran, the statue parades through Chiang Mai's streets in procession.

activities.text: On Sunday evenings, the Walking Street on Wualai Road comes alive from 5 PM. Local artisans sell hammered silver, Bo Sang umbrellas and hill tribe textiles - more local and less touristy than the standard night market. Food stalls offer khao soi (egg noodle curry, Northern specialty) and grilled Chiang Mai sausages. Plan at least 2 hours.

For those wanting to dive deeper into local cuisine, cooking classes are offered in the old city: morning market to choose ingredients, then 3 to 4 hours in the kitchen. Khao soi, green curry and tom kha are program classics. Book the day before.

map_keywords: Wat Phra That Doi Suthep Chiang Mai

visits.text: Leave early for Doi Suthep - 15 km from the city at 1,073 m altitude. The legend of its 1383 founding: a white elephant carrying a Buddha relic climbed the mountain, trumpeted three times and collapsed. The king saw this as a sign and built the temple. Climb the 309 steps flanked by nagas or take the funicular. At the top, the 24 m golden chedi gleams in the morning sun. The view over Chiang Mai from the terrace covers the entire plain. Arrive before 9 AM to avoid tour groups.

activities.text: In the afternoon, let yourself drift toward Nimman Road, Chiang Mai's café and creator quarter. Shophouses host local designer boutiques, galleries, coworking spaces and specialty coffee shops - Northern mountain coffee is a regional pride. One Nimman, the complex at the corner, groups craft shops, restaurants and smoothie bars. It's also the right place to find hill tribe textiles, celadon pottery and bamboo baskets.

map_keywords: Wat Rong Suea Ten Chiang Rai

visits.text: 13 km south of Chiang Rai, Wat Rong Khun resembles no other temple. Designed and financed by Thai artist Chalermchai Kositpipat from 1997, the project is still underway. Everything is white - encrusted with thousands of mirror fragments that make the façade shimmer like a water surface. The access bridge is lined with hands reaching up from the ground: they symbolize earthly desires the faithful leave behind. Inside, murals blend Buddhism with modern references - Superman, the Twin Towers, movie characters. Go early: morning light makes the mirrors sparkle.

6 km north of town, Wat Rong Suea Ten - Dancing Tigers Temple - completed in 2016. Here everything is deep sapphire blue highlighted with gold. The architect is a former student of Kositpipat. Blue symbolizes wisdom and abandonment of material attachments. Free entry, allow 45 minutes.

Baan Dam - the Black House - lies 10 km north of the center. National artist Thawan Duchanee devoted 50 years of work here until his death in 2014. Not a temple: an ensemble of 40 black-painted buildings, constructed in solid teak within a planted garden. The main building's interior is covered with animal skins, skulls and sculptures - a Buddhist aesthetic exploring death as life's hidden face. Unsettling, but without equivalent.

activities.text: In the evening, head to Chiang Rai's Night Bazaar. Calmer than Chiang Mai's, it's a human-scale market with hill tribe crafts, textiles and street food stalls. Order khao soi, grilled Chiang Mai sausages and mango sticky rice.

visits.text: Drive north to the Golden Triangle at Sop Ruak (65 km, 1h15). This is the convergence point of Thailand, Laos and Burma, marked by the Mekong. The name dates from the 1960s-90s, when this region produced most of the world's heroin. The Hall of Opium, a 5,500 m² museum financed under royal patronage, traces opium history from antiquity through the Sino-British opium wars, drug lords and royal hill country crop substitution programs. The multimedia rooms are striking. Allow at least 1.5 hours.

activities.text: Evening flight from Chiang Rai airport (CEI) to Bangkok, then connection to Krabi (KBV) the same evening or next morning. Check the connection when booking - some itineraries require an overnight in Bangkok. Book both flights well in advance.

practical_info.duration: Flight CEI → BKK: approximately 1h10, then BKK → KBV: 1h20

visits.text: Arrival at Krabi airport (KBV), then transfer to Ao Nang - the ideal base, 20 minutes by taxi. It's the region's most accessible beach: karst cliffs plunge directly into the turquoise Andaman Sea. Longtail boats at anchor, waterside vendors, coconut stands. This is a transit day - lay the groundwork and explore the waterfront.

activities.text: In the evening, dine on Ao Nang beach: several restaurants set up tables directly on the sand with cliff views. Order tom yum goong (spicy shrimp soup) or a whole grilled fish. Krabi Town's night market, 5 km away, also warrants the trip for a more local evening - pad thai stalls, banana sweets and fresh fruit juices.

visits.text: Board a longtail from Ao Nang (15-minute crossing) for Railay. This peninsula is cut off from the mainland by vertical limestone cliffs - accessible only by sea. West Railay facing the beach, East Railay facing mangroves, and at the end of the path Phra Nang beach, framed by rock pillars rising from the water. Phra Nang cave houses a shrine to the fishermen's goddess - an altar laden with floral offerings and votive phalluses, a thousand-year tradition among the region's sailors. Railay's cliffs are one of Southeast Asia's most renowned climbing spots. Operators offer day-long introductions.

Late afternoon, return to Krabi Town for Tiger Cave Temple. The main temple sits at the hill's base. But the summit is what's worth it: 1,237 steep steps through jungle, monitored by macaques. At the top, a golden Buddha, a chedi and panoramic views over Krabi plain, the limestone cliffs and the Andaman Sea beyond. Go late in the day to avoid heat - and plan 1.5 hours round trip.

activities.text: Kayak on the Sok River and wildlife watching
Evening at Fisherman's Village, Bophut

visits.text: Ferry from Krabi Town or Ao Nang to Koh Phi Phi Don (approximately 1h30). Upon arrival at Tonsai Village, drop your bags and climb to the viewpoint. Count 343 steps to the first belvedere - a few dozen more to the second. At the top: the view of Phi Phi's two bays, Tonsai Bay on the left, Loh Dalum Bay on the right, and the limestone cliffs enclosing them. It's the only spot on the island where you truly grasp its geography - a 150 m strip of land between two bays. Sunrise or late afternoon for the best light.

activities.text: The evening on Phi Phi Don is festive without being solely that. Tonsai Village offers restaurants on stilts, beachside cocktail bars and live music on Loh Dalum beach. Eat grilled fish by the water, drink a cocktail from a coconut shell. The vibe picks up around 10 PM - it's an island of backpackers and divers, relaxed atmosphere.

visits.text: Leave before 8 AM for the Maya Bay excursion. The Phi Phi Leh beach was made famous by Danny Boyle's film The Beach (2000) with Leonardo DiCaprio. Closed four years to recover from over-tourism, it reopened in 2022 with strict rules: no swimming, stay limited to 1 hour, access via floating dock at Loh Samah Bay then 10 minutes on foot.
